params.text:
Corpus:

The most useful research tool you didn't know you needed!
Go through these tasks step by step. This task should give you confidence to get started using a corpus on your own or as preparation for attending a workshop.
Next>>

params.text:
What is a corpus

Watch this brief video introduction to get a sense of what a corpus is and what can be done with it.
Next>>

Corpus: A brief overview

A corpus is a representative collection of texts, that can be used to research how people speak and what they say about different things.

Students and academics can also use a corpus to help them with writing and word choice, to supplement or even replace usage guides.

There are free corpora for many languages on the web and it is really easy to search through one. There are also specialist corpora for specific domains of interest, such as child language, historical English or translation between several languages
Corpus definition >>

Task 1: Choose a Corpus

There are many corpora but the best starter for most English research is English-Corpora.org. 

It offers many options but the best to start with is the iWeb corpus.

elements.solution: This is one of the many corpora available on English-corpora.org. It is best to get started with for general users but lacks some of the more advanced features such as comparison.

params.text: Next>>
Launch iWeb

Note for later:

You only get a few free searches before you are asked to register. When you create your account don't forget to associate it with Oxford University to get more free usage.

Task 2: How do people describe X ?

Find out what are the most common ways to complete a phrase, by searching for a phrase using a wildcard. eg. school is like a *
Next>>
Did you get the same top 20 results? >>

params.alt: School is like a - search results
params.title: Example of search results

params.text:
* stands for any string of characters including the whole word
? stands for any one character

keywords.main: Task 2: How do people describe X ?

params.text:
Task 3: What is a typical collocate of Y ?

A collocate is a word that appears frequently with another. This can give you a good indication of what how the word is used and the associations people may have with it.
Next>>
Did you get the same top 20 results? >>

Step 1: Click + at the top of the Search panel and choose Collocates

Step 2: Type in the word 'climate' in the Word/phrase box and click

Step 3: Review the list of words/phrases that appear with climate, and their frequencies

Note: Try using the blue numbers to specify words before/after the search term (and distance)

keywords.main: Task 3: What is a typical collocate of Y?

params.text:
Task 4: What is the most common spelling of …

You can also search for different spellings of a word or its other uses
Find out whether travelling or traveling is a more common spelling.
Next>>
Did you get the same top 20 results? >>

params.alt: School is like a - search results
params.title: Example of search results

params.text: Hint: use * or ? in your search.

Do you want to compare genres, countries or periods? 

Corpora are great for comparing usage in different contexts but the iWeb corpus does not let you do that directly. You have to use one of the other corpora on English-Corpora.org.

keywords.main: Task 4:  What is the most common spelling of …

params.text: BNC - British National Corpus
Note: Comparing genres, countries and time periods
Next>>
COCA -   Corpus of Contemporary American English
GloWbE - Corpus of Global Web-Based English

COCA - Corpus of Contemporary American English

This was one of the first corpora released as part of this project. It remains a good guide to general features of English.

It covers English from 1990-2019 and includes 600 million words - which now makes it a mid-size corpus..

GloWbE - Global Web-based English

This corpus covers a snapshot of the web in 2012-2014 covering pages from 20 countries with a total of almost 2 billion words.

BNC - British National Corpus

This is mostly a legacy corpus - it was developed as part of a separate UK project in the 1990s. But it is the best resource if your aim is purely British English. It only covers language from the 1980s to 1993 and with "only" 100 million words, it is a smaller-size non-specialised corpus.

Corpus is great for comparing usage in different contexts but the iWeb corpus does not let you do that directly. Some of the other corpora on English-Corpora.org give you that option in the interface.

params.text: Search
Result

params.alt: Using the iWeb as a dictionary and thesaurus
params.title: Using the iWeb as a dictionary and thesaurus

params.text:
What are the most common 2-letter endings used with ‘school’? (hint: use ?)

Using the ? wild card is very useful, if you want to find out about forms of words. In this case, when you search for school??, you can see that there are only a few legitimate two-letter endings that can be used. The others are simply errors.

What verbs are used immediately after the word school?

  • You can limit the search for any word by its part of speech: noun, verb, adjective.
  • To do this, simply click on the part of speech selector. 
  • You can search for all verbs following the word school as in 'school _v*' or you can search for all the times school is used as a verb as in 'school_v*'
  • The part of speech labels are automatically generated at about 98% accuracy. You still have to use your judgement when evaluating specific examples.

Try KWIC view  to see how a different layout can help you get a better insight into your results. 

  • KWIC view stands for Key Word in Context and it is the standard way of displaying corpus search results in linguistic research.
  • The KWIC view lets you quickly get a sense for how word or phrase is actually used.
  • The English-Corpora.org KWIC view is even more useful by colour-coding part of speech of the neibouring words.

Try browse for words to see what usage guidance a corpus can give you

  • Browse or Word search essentially turns in the iWeb corpus into an advanced dictionary combined with a thesaurus and a usage guide. You can not only see the meanings of the word but also its common usage patterns.
  • This feature is only availble on the iWeb corpus and not the other corpora using the same system.

Optional task: Try other corpora

English-Corpora.org offers a number of specialised corpora that may be useful for different purposes. There is COHA for historical English, the Hansard corpus for English politics, TV corpus for spoken language, TIME Magazine corpus for developments in American English and more.

Try these corpora to see if they could help you with your work.
Next>>

Optional task: Try even more corpora

There are many more corpora to explore if you need even more specialised data. They will have slightly different usage rules and interfaces.

  • Corpora in other languages: All major languages will have an online corpus available. English Corpora has a Spanish counterpart in Corpusdelespanol.org. There are also parallel corpora where you can compare translations.
  • Corpora of specific types of language: CHILDES (corpus of child language), TalkBank (corpora of spoken languages), corpus of student writing, corpus of writing aimed at children, etc.
Next>>

keywords.main: Optional task: Even more specialised corpora

params.text:
Optional task for teachers: Try Lextutor

Lextutor.ca takes the corpus data and constructs learning exercises that can be helpful to students in acquiring academic English. 

Try the Multi Concordance tool (under Concordance) to see how you can make an exercise to practice the use of words and phrases specific to academic English.
Next>>
How to set it up>>
Next steps: Want to start using a corpus or learn more?
Next>>

Step 1: Sign up for Corpus workshop

We are offering a 90 minute workshop where you can learn more about how it can be used as well as how to compile your own.

Step 2: Have a look at the Bodelian libguides on Corpora

Bodelian maintain a guide about corpora with links to resources available at the university and elsewhere.
Deep dive: Explore even further
Summary>>

Corpora across languages: Sign up for Sketchengine

Sketchengine is a collection of corpora across many uses and languages. It has a unified interface and supports research and learning. Oxford University members can access it for free.

There is also a publicly available version for learners SKELL.

Learn about corpus research: How are frequencies calculated

This video and related materials will give you a quick intro to the key principles of corpus statistics. It is useful to understand how frequencies and collocabilities are calculated even if you don't do statistics yourself.

params.text:
Summary

Choose the right statement to get a summary of what everyone should know about corpora.

summary: A corpus is a representative collection of texts in a language.
A corpus is a collection of all texts available in the language.
A corpus is the same as Google for linguists. It search the whole web.
A corpus can be used to research how people speak and what they say about different things.
A corpus is primarily used to find information on the web.
Students and academics can also use a corpus to help them with writing to supplement or even replace usage guides.
A corpus is used by editors to find errors in texts.
There are free corpora for many languages on the web and it is really easy to search through one.
Corpora are highly technical and very expensive to use.
There are also specialist corpora for specific domains of interest, such as child language, historical English or translation between several languages.
Corpora only exist for contemporary English and are limited to journalism.
